summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

If a data set is normally distributed with mean 50 and standard deviation 5, what is the z-score of the value 60?

The z-score formula is z = (x - mean) / standard deviation = (60 - 50) / 5 = 10/5 = 2. This means 60 is 2 standard deviations above the mean.

In a normal distribution, what is the shape of the curve?

A normal distribution curve is symmetric and bell-shaped, centered at the mean. The tails extend infinitely in both directions but never touch the x-axis.

A test score of 72 has a z-score of -0.5 when the mean is 80. What is the standard deviation?

Using z = (x - mean) / sd: -0.5 = (72 - 80) / sd = -8 / sd. Solving: sd = -8 / -0.5 = 16. The standard deviation is 16.

What is a z-score?

A z-score measures how many standard deviations a particular data value is away from the mean. It standardizes data values so they can be compared across different normal distributions.

What is the standard normal distribution?

The standard normal distribution is a specific normal distribution with mean (mu) equal to 0 and standard deviation (sigma) equal to 1. It is used as a reference distribution for calculating probabilities.

What is a percentile in the context of normal distributions?

A percentile indicates the percentage of data values in a distribution that fall at or below a given value. For example, the 75th percentile means 75% of the data falls at or below that value.

What is the total area under a normal distribution curve?

The total area under any normal distribution curve is exactly 1 (or 100%). This represents the certainty that a data value falls somewhere within the distribution.

Students' scores on a standardized exam are normally distributed with a mean of 500 and standard deviation of 100. A student scored 680. What is the student's z-score?

z = (x - mean) / sd = (680 - 500) / 100 = 180/100 = 1.8. The student scored 1.8 standard deviations above the mean, which is a strong performance.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: If a data set is normally distributed with mean 50 and standard deviation 5, what is the z-score of the value 60?

The z-score formula is z = (x - mean) / standard deviation = (60 - 50) / 5 = 10/5 = 2. This means 60 is 2 standard deviations above the mean.

Example: What percentage of data in a normal distribution falls within two standard deviations of the mean?

According to the empirical rule, approximately 95% of data in a normal distribution falls within two standard deviations of the mean (between the mean minus 2 sigma and the mean plus 2 sigma).

Example: A normal distribution has mean 100 and standard deviation 15. What value has a z-score of -1?

Using x = mean + z * standard deviation: x = 100 + (-1)(15) = 100 - 15 = 85. A z-score of -1 means the value is one standard deviation below the mean.

Example: Approximately what percentage of data falls within three standard deviations of the mean in a normal distribution?

The empirical rule states that approximately 99.7% of data falls within three standard deviations of the mean. This means only about 0.3% of data lies beyond three standard deviations.

Example: A test score of 72 has a z-score of -0.5 when the mean is 80. What is the standard deviation?

Using z = (x - mean) / sd: -0.5 = (72 - 80) / sd = -8 / sd. Solving: sd = -8 / -0.5 = 16. The standard deviation is 16.

Example: What is a z-score?

A z-score measures how many standard deviations a particular data value is away from the mean. It standardizes data values so they can be compared across different normal distributions.

Example: What does a negative z-score indicate?

A negative z-score indicates that the data value is below the mean. The magnitude of the z-score tells how many standard deviations below the mean the value lies.

Example: A factory produces light bulbs with lifespans that are normally distributed with a mean of 1200 hours and a standard deviation of 100 hours. What percentage of bulbs last between 1000 and 1400 hours?

1000 hours is 2 standard deviations below the mean (z = -2) and 1400 hours is 2 standard deviations above (z = 2). By the empirical rule, approximately 95% of data falls within 2 standard deviations of the mean.

Example: Students' scores on a standardized exam are normally distributed with a mean of 500 and standard deviation of 100. A student scored 680. What is the student's z-score?

z = (x - mean) / sd = (680 - 500) / 100 = 180/100 = 1.8. The student scored 1.8 standard deviations above the mean, which is a strong performance.

Example: A company's daily sales are normally distributed with a mean of $5,000 and a standard deviation of $800. On what percentage of days do sales fall below $3,400?

z = (3400 - 5000) / 800 = -1600/800 = -2. By the empirical rule, about 95% of data is within 2 standard deviations, so about 5% is outside, and about 2.5% is below z = -2.

Example: The heights of adult women in a city are normally distributed with mean 64 inches and standard deviation 3 inches. Maria's height has a z-score of 2. How tall is Maria?

Using x = mean + z * sd: x = 64 + 2(3) = 64 + 6 = 70 inches. Maria is 70 inches (5 feet 10 inches) tall, which is 2 standard deviations above the average.

Example: A tire company claims their tires last an average of 60,000 miles with a standard deviation of 5,000 miles, normally distributed. If a customer's tire lasts 47,500 miles, how many standard deviations below the mean is this?

z = (47500 - 60000) / 5000 = -12500 / 5000 = -2.5. The tire lasted 2.5 standard deviations below the mean, which is an unusually short lifespan.

Example: A school's math test scores are normally distributed with a mean of 75 and standard deviation of 10. A student wants to score in the top 16% of the class. What minimum score does the student need?

The top 16% starts at approximately z = 1 (since 84% is below z = 1). The score is x = 75 + 1(10) = 85. Scoring 85 or above places the student in approximately the top 16%.

Example: Two students take different standardized tests. Alex scores 82 on a test with mean 75 and standard deviation 4. Jordan scores 90 on a test with mean 80 and standard deviation 6. Who performed better relative to their peers?

Alex's z-score: (82-75)/4 = 1.75. Jordan's z-score: (90-80)/6 = 1.67. Since 1.75 > 1.67, Alex performed better relative to peers despite having a lower raw score.

Example: A coffee shop brews cups of coffee whose temperatures are normally distributed with a mean of 175 degrees F and a standard deviation of 8 degrees F. Approximately what percentage of cups are served between 159 and 191 degrees F?

z for 159: (159-175)/8 = -2. z for 191: (191-175)/8 = 2. The range from z = -2 to z = 2 contains approximately 95% of the data by the empirical rule.

Example: A delivery company's packages arrive with delivery times that are normally distributed with a mean of 5 days and a standard deviation of 1 day. The company guarantees delivery within 7 days or a refund. Using the empirical rule, approximately what percentage of packages will require a refund?

7 days is 2 standard deviations above the mean (z = 2). About 95% of deliveries are within 2 standard deviations, so about 5% are outside. Since we only care about late deliveries (above z = 2), that is approximately 2.5%.
